response:
## Decision: O4 — Pre-registered sequential escalation

**Adopt O4.** Run all 4 arms (Haiku-solo, Sonnet-solo, Opus-solo, Tiered) at the pre-registered minimal design, check per-cell/eval-clustered CIs against the decision boundary, and add reps/tasks **only** where a CI straddles it — stopping once the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo call is unambiguous. Do not front-load the full ~250-300-invocation grid unconditionally, and do not narrow to a 2-arm pilot (O2/O3).

### Why O4 over O1

All three advisors independently converged on 5/5 for O4 and 2/2/2 for O1 with no second round needed (spread < 2 on both). The live judgment call was whether Comment 3 — "the pre-registration validated against actual code" — silently superseded Comment 1's explicit Analysis-section clause ("add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous"), which is the shared reversal condition on both sides of this pair.

It doesn't supersede it, for three reasons:
1. **Scope mismatch, not repeal.** Comment 3 is titled "Execution design" and its content is entirely about code/cost: arm list, task count, "New code needed" (one orchestration script), and the ~250-300 invocation estimate for owner budget sign-off. Sequential escalation isn't a code artifact — checking a CI against a boundary and deciding whether to run more is a manual/analysis-time decision using the same `aggregate.py`/blind-grade tooling already reused elsewhere. Its absence from a "new code needed" list is not evidence it was dropped; escalation was never going to appear there.
2. **No comment says so.** Superseding a pre-registered falsifiable-design clause — the exact mechanism registration exists to make credible — should require an explicit statement, not silent inference from omission in a narrower-scoped comment. Reading silence as repeal is itself the kind of after-the-fact convenience the pre-registration was written to prevent (Comment 1: "Registered before running to avoid fishing for a favorable cut").
3. **The "~250-300 invocations" figure is a budget ceiling, not a mandate to spend it all upfront.** It's presented under "Owner calls before spend" alongside grader-independence and hermetic-control questions — pre-spend judgment calls, not a statement that the full grid runs unconditionally before any check.

So O1's framing ("all 4 arms... at 3 reps each **before any analysis**") is the reading advisors flag as spending more than what's actually registered, and O4 is the faithful execution of the standing pre-registration.

### Why not O2 / O3

Both scored 1-3 across all advisors and fail the same structural test: Comment 1's Method section pre-registers all 4 arms together, and the Decision rule explicitly requires reporting whether Tiered reaches "Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost" ("the real prize") *regardless* of the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo outcome. A 2-arm pilot (Tiered+Sonnet-solo for O2, or Opus-solo+Sonnet-solo for O3) cannot produce that required report and abandons the quality-cost frontier the design is built around. Their reversal conditions (Opus-prize reporting being merely optional, or prize-reachability gating spend on Tiered) aren't supported anywhere in Comment 1 or Comment 3 — the decision rule and prize-reporting line are stated as flat requirements, not conditional ones.

### Net effect
Execute Comment 3's 4-arm/8-task design starting from the pre-registered minimal rep count, with the Analysis-section escalation clause governing whether/where to add reps or tasks, and stopping the moment the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo call plus the Opus-prize check are both unambiguous — rather than always paying for the full grid or narrowing to a subset of arms that can't answer the registered questions.

This is a judgment call under a live ADR-governed process (ADR 0024 cost-justification, ADR 0019 grading). Per this repo's PDCA convention, if you want this formalized as an ADR entry in `docs/decisions/` rather than just this reply, say so and I'll draft it — I haven't created a file since you asked for the record in-reply.

options.label: Full pre-registered grid
options.summary: Run all 4 arms (Haiku-solo, Sonnet-solo, Opus-solo, Tiered) across all 8 pre-screened tasks at 3 reps each before any analysis.

options.label: Pilot Tiered arm first
options.summary: Run Tiered vs Sonnet-solo baseline first at pre-registered reps; check the delta/token decision rule; stop before scaling other arms if already decidable.

options.label: Pilot Opus-solo first
options.summary: Run the costliest-per-invocation arm, Opus-solo, plus Sonnet-solo baseline first to test reachability of the 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' prize before full-grid spend.

options.label: Pre-registered sequential escalation
options.summary: Run all 4 arms at minimal reps first, adding reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary, per Comment 1's stated escalation method.

O1.crux: Running the full grid 'before any analysis' ignores Comment 1's explicit Analysis-section instruction to add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the boundary — it's a bigger upfront spend than what was actually pre-registered.
O1.dependency: If Comment 3's flat 3-reps/8-tasks/4-arms design (validated against actual code, no incremental-analysis tooling in its 'New code needed' list) is the as-built plan that superseded the escalation clause, O1 is simply the registered execution and this reverses upward.

O2.crux: Piloting only Tiered vs Sonnet-solo and stopping before Haiku-solo/Opus-solo is an execution order found nowhere in the pre-registration — exactly the after-the-fact convenience ordering Comment 1 says registration exists to prevent, and it risks skipping the mandatory 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' report.
O2.dependency: If the pre-registered decision rule genuinely depends on nothing but Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo (true per its literal text) and the Opus-prize comparison is optional secondary reporting rather than a required output, stopping after just those two arms becomes a defensible reading of 'decidable' and this reverses upward.

O3.crux: Leading with Opus-solo tests the secondary 'real prize' question, not the actual pre-registered decision rule (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo delta/tokens) — another ordering absent from any comment and orthogonal to what triggers adoption.
O3.dependency: If the owner's true priority is reachability of Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost (the original issue's framing) over the formal non-inferiority test, front-loading Opus-solo is the right prioritization and this reverses upward.

O4.crux: This is verbatim Comment 1's registered Analysis method ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous') — it's executing the pre-registration as written, not deviating from it.
O4.dependency: If Comment 3's actual-code validation quietly dropped the escalation clause (it restates the grid and invocation count with no mention of staged/incremental analysis machinery), the as-built pipeline may not support escalation at all, reversing this downward.

O1.crux: Runs the full 3-rep grid unconditionally, dropping Comment 1's explicit pre-registered escalation/stop clause ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the boundary; stop when unambiguous') rather than just executing it.
O1.dependency: If per-cell CIs can't be meaningfully checked before all 3 reps land (i.e. the escalation add-on has no smaller 'minimal' unit to start from), O1 collapses into the same base run O4 prescribes and its score rises.

O2.crux: Tests only the arm pair the headline decision rule cites (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo), but Comment 1's Method section pre-registers all 4 arms together, including the pre-screen-established Haiku<Opus gradient the whole design leans on.
O2.dependency: If the Opus-solo/Haiku-solo arms are genuinely secondary (only feeding the 'also report' prize line, not the adopt/reject rule), skipping them first is a legitimate reading of 'escalate only where needed' and the score should rise.

O3.crux: Front-loads the secondary 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' prize question and skips the Tiered arm entirely, so the pilot can't resolve the actual pre-registered adoption decision (which hinges on Tiered vs Sonnet-solo).
O3.dependency: If the owner treats prize-reachability as a gating precondition for spending on Tiered at all (rather than a secondary report line, as Comment 1 frames it), this ordering becomes defensible and the score should rise.

O4.crux: Directly restates Comment 1's own 'Sequential escalation' analysis clause (minimal reps across all 4 arms first, escalate only where a CI straddles the boundary) — the most faithful, least-improvised execution of the pre-registration.
O4.dependency: If Comment 3 (the later, code-validated execution design) silently supersedes that clause with a flat 3-reps-for-every-cell plan and no minimal-first step, then the 'pre-registered' method today is really O1, and O4 no longer matches current pre-registration — reversing the score downward.

O1.crux: Runs the full ~250-300 invocation grid even when the pre-registered decision gate might already be decidable after fewer reps, ignoring Comment 1's own 'sequential escalation' clause.
O1.dependency: Reverses to 4-5 if Comment 1's fixed '3 replicates per (task x arm)' Method sentence is the binding pre-registration and the Analysis section's escalation line is only advisory guidance for extending past it, not a mandate to start smaller.

O2.crux: Targets the actual pre-registered decision rule (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo delta/tokens) and can stop early, but drops Haiku-solo and Opus-solo entirely rather than running all 4 arms at minimal reps, losing the quality-cost frontier and the 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' prize check Comment 1 requires reporting regardless.
O2.dependency: Reverses to 4-5 if the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo decision rule is judged the sole adoption gate, making the 4-arm frontier and Opus-prize reporting optional rather than a required deliverable of this run.

O3.crux: Pilots Opus-solo + Sonnet-solo but omits Tiered — the arm actually under test — so it cannot assess whether Tiered reaches 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost,' and piloting the single costliest arm first is backwards for an early-stop cost-saving strategy.
O3.dependency: Reverses only if the owner's goal for this pilot is narrowly a go/no-go on whether Opus-quality is even worth chasing before paying the cost of building the new tiered-orchestration script, i.e. that script doesn't exist yet.

O4.crux: Matches Comment 1's pre-registration verbatim ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous') while preserving all 4 arms for the quality-cost frontier and Opus-prize check.
O4.dependency: Reverses if Comment 3's later, concrete execution design ('3 reps per (task x arm)', no minimal-then-escalate mention) is read as having superseded and finalized away from Comment 1's sequential-escalation clause.

O1.crux: Running the full grid 'before any analysis' ignores Comment 1's explicit Analysis-section instruction to add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the boundary — it's a bigger upfront spend than what was actually pre-registered.
O1.dependency: If Comment 3's flat 3-reps/8-tasks/4-arms design (validated against actual code, no incremental-analysis tooling in its 'New code needed' list) is the as-built plan that superseded the escalation clause, O1 is simply the registered execution and this reverses upward.

O2.crux: Piloting only Tiered vs Sonnet-solo and stopping before Haiku-solo/Opus-solo is an execution order found nowhere in the pre-registration — exactly the after-the-fact convenience ordering Comment 1 says registration exists to prevent, and it risks skipping the mandatory 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' report.
O2.dependency: If the pre-registered decision rule genuinely depends on nothing but Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo (true per its literal text) and the Opus-prize comparison is optional secondary reporting rather than a required output, stopping after just those two arms becomes a defensible reading of 'decidable' and this reverses upward.

O3.crux: Leading with Opus-solo tests the secondary 'real prize' question, not the actual pre-registered decision rule (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo delta/tokens) — another ordering absent from any comment and orthogonal to what triggers adoption.
O3.dependency: If the owner's true priority is reachability of Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost (the original issue's framing) over the formal non-inferiority test, front-loading Opus-solo is the right prioritization and this reverses upward.

O4.crux: This is verbatim Comment 1's registered Analysis method ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous') — it's executing the pre-registration as written, not deviating from it.
O4.dependency: If Comment 3's actual-code validation quietly dropped the escalation clause (it restates the grid and invocation count with no mention of staged/incremental analysis machinery), the as-built pipeline may not support escalation at all, reversing this downward.

O1.crux: Runs the full 3-rep grid unconditionally, dropping Comment 1's explicit pre-registered escalation/stop clause ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the boundary; stop when unambiguous') rather than just executing it.
O1.dependency: If per-cell CIs can't be meaningfully checked before all 3 reps land (i.e. the escalation add-on has no smaller 'minimal' unit to start from), O1 collapses into the same base run O4 prescribes and its score rises.

O2.dependency: If the Opus-solo/Haiku-solo arms are genuinely secondary (only feeding the 'also report' prize line, not the adopt/reject rule), skipping them first is a legitimate reading of 'escalate only where needed' and the score should rise.
